narrative: As a Black male applicant with a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a solid LSAT score of 39, you brought impressive academic credentials to your law school application, along with your upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 73.8% chance that you would fail it, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of poor bar passage outcomes for the school. This prediction hinged most heavily on your school decile rankings from Years 1 and 3, both at the 1st decile—far below the averages of around the 5th or 6th decile—indicating early and persistent struggles with grades relative to your peers. Your Year 3 decile had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, as it signaled ongoing academic challenges that the model sees as a major red flag for bar success. Your Year 1 decile reinforced this concern, pushing the prediction further in the same direction by highlighting that these issues started early in your education. On a brighter note, your high undergraduate GPA worked against the failure prediction, helping to offset some of those concerns and showing your potential in a college setting. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your lower school deciles stand out as unusually weak, while your GPA aligns more with successful applicants, creating a mixed but ultimately concerning profile. The model weighs these school performance indicators heavily because they often correlate strongly with bar exam readiness in its analysis of past students. This isn't a judgment on your character or background as a Black man from an upper-middle-income family—it's purely about how these academic patterns matched historical data. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, perhaps through community college or targeted prep that addresses whatever held you back early on. Strengthening those foundational metrics could flip the model's outlook, and pairing it with your already strong GPA and LSAT might open doors at other schools. You're clearly capable, as your undergrad record shows—focusing on consistent high school-level performance could make all the difference next time.
